data.title: Tesla, Boeing, IBM, And General Electric Head Into The Earnings Confessional
data.description:
Get ahead of the market by subscribing to Seeking Alpha's Wall Street Week Ahead, a preview of key events scheduled for the coming week. The newsletter keeps you informed of the biggest stories set to make headlines, including upcoming IPOs, investor days, earnings reports, and conference presentations.

Investors next week will be looking at existing- and new-home sales reports as well as the latest durable-goods data ahead of the Fed’s next meeting on November 7. Some 90% of market participants expect the Federal Open Market Committee to cut its benchmark interest rate by 25 basis points while nearly 10% expect no change, according to the CME Group’s FedWatch Tool. Meanwhile, the Bank of Canada is expected to cut interest rates by 50 basis points amid signs of a cooling economy, according to a poll of economists by Reuters. Aggregate earnings are expected to reach a new all-time high for the second straight quarter, with a current estimate of $511.4 billion in Q3, surpassing last quarter’s actual figure of $504.8 billion, per data from Lipper Alpha Insight. However, Q3 earnings were noted to face tougher year-over-year comparisons, resulting in an expected growth rate of 5.0%, down from the 13.2% seen last quarter. Analysts expect the energy sector to see some of the sharpest cuts in guidance alongside the quarterly corporate earnings updates.

The earnings season picks up heat in the week ahead with a focus on industrials, with Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA), Boeing (NYSE:BA), GE Aerospace (GE), Honeywell International (HON), and United Parcel Service (UPS).

Earnings spotlight: Monday, October 21 - Nucor (NUE) and Logitech (LOGI).

Earnings spotlight: Tuesday, October 22 - General Electric (GE), Philip Morris (PM), Verizon (VZ), Texas Instruments (TXN), Lockheed Martin (LMT), General Motors (GM), and 3M (MMM). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Wednesday, October 23 - Tesla (TSLA), Coca-Cola (KO), T-Mobile (TMUS), IBM (NYSE:IBM), AT&T (T), and Boeing (BA). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Thursday, October 24 - Valero (VLO), UPS (UPS), Honeywell (HON), American Airlines (AAL), Northrop Grumman (NOC), Southwest Airlines (LUV), and Dow (DOW). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Friday, October 25 - Centene (CNC), HCA (HCA), and Colgate-Palmolive (CL). See the full earnings calendar.

Volatility watch: Options trading volume is elevated on Alto Neuroscience (ANRO) and NuScale Power (SMR). The most overbought stocks per their 14-day relative strength index include Arcadium Lithium (ALTM), Eton Pharmaceuticals (ETON), and Cal-Maine (CALM). The most oversold stocks per their 14-day Relative Strength Index include Bioceres Crop Solutions (BIOX), Stride (LRN), LiveOne (LVO). Short interest is elevated on Wheeler Real Estate Investment (WHLR) and Hertz Global Holdings (HTZ) as earnings approach.

IPO watch: HUHUTECH International (HUHU) is expected to begin trading Tuesday after pricing its IPO the day before. Ingram Micro Holding (INGM) will start trading on Thursday after pricing its IPO on Wednesday. The IPO lockup periods expire during the week for blocks of shares of Neo-Concept International (NCI), mF International (MFI), YY Group (YYGH), and CleanCore Solutions (ZONE).


Dividend watch: Companies that have an ex-dividend date coming next week include Caterpillar (CAT), CVS Health (CVS), Lowe’s (LOW), Clorox (CLX), and Fastenal (FAST). Companies forecast to increase their quarterly dividend payouts include Smurfit Westrock (SW) to $0.33 from $0.3025, Hartford Financial Services Group (HIG) to $0.50 from $0.47, Entergy (ETR) to $1.20 from $1.13, and American Electric Power (AEP) to $0.92 from $0.88. Read through some of the dividend stock picks from Seeking Alpha analysts.

Investor events: Qualcomm (QCOM) will hold its Snapdragon Summit in Hawaii Monday through Wednesday. The company is expected to highlight its Snapdragon processors. 13D Monitor will hold its 2024 Active-Passive Investor Summit in New York on Tuesday. The event’s speakers will include Starboard Value Chief Executive Jeff Smith, Glass Lewis Senior Director of M&A Research Jason McCandless, and International Business Machines (IBM) Vice Chair Gary Cohn. Notable investor events during the week include Jefferies (JEF), McCormick and Co. (MKC), Beyond (BYON), and The Container Store (TCS).

Tesla earnings preview: Tesla (TSLA) will report earnings on Wednesday. Analysts expect the EV maker to post revenue of $29.73B and EPS of $0.86. Gross margin is expected to come in at 18.77%. The Elon Musk-led firm will also hold its earnings conference call. A point of major emphasis will be his commentary on the timing of a new mass-market cheaper electric vehicle, as well as the latest updates on FSD, energy storage business, and robotaxi developments.

UPS earnings preview: United Parcel Service (UPS) will report its results on Thursday. Analysts expect the shipping giant to post revenue of $22.16B and adjusted EPS of $1.63.

data.title: Trade Alert: My Top Canadian Bank Pays High Dividends - Scotiabank
data.description:
Co-authored with Treading Softly

My Favorite Canadian Bank

When it comes to depositing money into your bank, few of us think twice about the overall stability and health of the financial institution that we're depositing into. If you're living within the United States, you understand that there is an FDIC insurance should the bank go belly up, covering a certain amount of your deposits. You would always be wise to maintain your deposits to be less than the insurance coverage, sometimes forcing you to deposit into multiple banks if you have a higher net wealth overall.

When we look north of the border to the Canadian economy, many investors suddenly get confused at the lack of bank options to invest in or use as your financial institution. The big difference is that while the Canadian and American banking structures seem similar, many differences are below the surface.

The biggest initial difference is that unlike the United States, which is more willing to hand out bank charters to small, one-off banks or financial institutions, allowing a large realm of competition, Canada took a more hands-on approach.

Currently, there are 4470 FDIC-insured financial institutions in the United States. In Canada, there are 35 domestic banks-financial institutions that are not a subsidiary of a foreign company but are domiciled in Canada. In addition, there are 15 foreign banks that are subsidiaries of other firms.

Much like the American system, Canadian banks are covered by something called CDIC, which operates essentially the same as the FDIC but is in Canada. However, the big difference is that in Canada, the six largest banks have been labeled as being so economically important to the functioning of Canada that they are not allowed to collapse, and are run throughout like a regular bank board. This means that if there is a bank run, the government itself would step in to either oversee the transition of the firm or the health of the firm because it would have an overreaching economic impact. The big six banks in Canada are the primary controllers of the financial sector. We often hear about big American banks having an outsize impact, like Bank of America (BAC), Citibank (C), or Wells Fargo (WFC). But the impact on the Canadian financial system from these six banks is stronger than their American counterparts, at least an impact confined to Canada itself.

These big six are:

  • Royal Bank of Canada (RY)
  • Toronto Dominion Bank (TD)
  • Bank of Montreal (BMO)
  • Bank of Nova Scotia (NYSE:BNS)
  • Canadian Imperial Bank of Commerce (CM)
  • National Bank of Canada (NA:CA)

When looking at places to generate strong income over the long run, I want to take a moment and examine my favorite Canadian bank, which happens to be one of these big six. We will look at why I think that this is a great place to be able to generate income for the long run while also enjoying a higher yield than available from the other five big banks.

Let's dive in!

Let's Go Out East!

The Bank of Nova Scotia (BNS), also commonly known as Scotiabank, was founded in the 1800s. At that time, Nova Scotia was not a province of Canada but was a standalone colony that Britain had founded. As time passed, the bank continued to expand across Canada and became the third-largest financial institution within Canada. BNS offers all of the standard fare as far as banking goes with commercial, personal, and lending activity that one would expect. This means that, in essence, you can readily compare it to an American financial institution with all of the options that are available, as well as all of the considerations for how it should be performing.

Financial Results

When it comes to operating a successful financial institution, we want to see consistent EPS growth, meaning that the shares that they've issued are seeing more income every year from the successful operations.

We also want to see steady growth in overall revenue because we're expecting BNS to see that top-line number grow over time, even if it is issuing more shares. Secondly, we also want to see a consistent or sustainable profit margin-growth at any cost is not a viable strategy for a company we want to use as an income investment. This means top-line growth alone, without growth at the bottom line, would be a big red flag.

We can see that all the way up until about 2014, BNS was able to strongly provide growing EPS, growing revenue, and a solid profit margin. Once we exit 2014, we have had about a decade of slow growth or flat growth. We have to ask ourselves, why is this occurring, and do we expect that it will continue to occur? Because, if we expect continued slowness in overall growth, that would be a sign that maybe something that management is doing is ineffective, or is there an external cause that can impact the financial health? We must recognize that the big six banks in Canada are heavily impacted by any sort of economic changes or development within the Canadian economy itself. So, how has the Canadian economy done over the last ten years?

In essence, the Canadian economy's overall gross domestic product, GDP, has been flat. In 2014, Canada's GDP was $1.8 trillion; in 2023, it was $2.14 trillion-an overall change of 18%. To put this into perspective, the United States GDP change for the same period was 55.4%.

The prior decade, from 2004 to 2013, saw nearly a doubling in Canada's GDP from barely over a trillion to 1.8 trillion. This corresponds with the strong revenue growth that we saw within BNS. We can deduce that how the Canadian economy fares, so too will the major banks. Looking across the other major financial institutions in Canada, we see a similar result pattern.

One major differentiator that we can expect to see impacting BNS is their recent plan to become a major shareholder in KeyCorp (KEY). For the members of High Dividend Opportunities, we have covered this merger plan previously and have also discussed KEY preferreds several times.

Becoming a major shareholder gives BNS major exposure to the U.S. market at a highly attractive price, arguably at better multiples than Bank of Montreal's acquisition of Bank of the West. BNS already had exposure to the Mexican market, allowing them to seek growth there as well as expanding their exposure internationally.

The Dividend Income

BNS maintains an A+ credit rating from leading credit agencies and is ranked as the 26th safest bank in the world. The bank paid its first dividend in 1833 and has not missed a single quarterly dividend payment for over 191 years, making it one of the longest-uninterrupted dividend payers in North America.

When it comes to investing for income, the goal is to find an income stream that has a yield that we find highly attractive and livable, but also one that is sustainable or has strong growth prospects. With BNS, we get both.

For investors living in the United States, there are two key things to recognize here. First off, because BNS is located in Canada, its dividend payments are declared in Canadian dollars. So the appearance of variability will be caused by the exchange rate of the Canadian dollar to the American dollar, causing the dividend to appear to shift up and down even though it's been steadily growing in Canadian dollars. Secondly, if you hold BNS in a taxable account, there will be a 15% Canadian tax withholding, which you can then claim back on tax returns. When held in an IRA, there should be no tax withholding by your brokerage. The next ex-dividend will be in early January.

Looking Forward

When we look forward to BNS's future perspective, we can expect that the bank will continue to function well, so long as the Canadian economy likewise functions well. The major risks to the Canadian economy are major risks to BNS. Their investment in KEY and into the Latin financial markets do help provide additional diversity, but overwhelmingly, BNS's results will be impacted by the Canadian economy and its output.

We can see that the Canadian economy over the last decade has lagged heavily when compared to the United States economy, largely because of various political impacts. That's not something that we're going to dive deeply into today, but in essence, higher levels of taxation over the last decade have drained the GDP growth of the Canadian economy, funneling money out of the economy towards the government. Whether your opinion is for or against that is something that, unfortunately, we can't address here. What we can state is that if the economy in Canada finds a stronger footing, we can expect to see strong growth and strong dividend growth from BNS. If the economy continues to linger, we can continue to expect that the revenue growth within BNS will also linger, but still provide strong coverage to the common dividend payout.

Over the long run, betting on a bank is a good bet. When that bank has been deemed so financially and economically important to the country that they're not willing to let it fail, you're able to have a greater reassurance than buying a regional bank that has less stability provided to it by the government.

When it comes to retirement, having a steady stream of income is invaluable. You can't put a value on a growing income stream that provides you with the life-giving cash that you need day to day. I love to get income from sources that other people don't even think twice about. Your bank makes money every time you use your debit card, every single dollar that you deposit into it, and every time it originates a loan, and the borrower makes payments towards it. Banks provide financial tools and liquidity to the market and the economy that otherwise wouldn't exist without them. By buying into the 3rd largest bank in the Canadian economy, you can receive a strong and stable income stream that is growing and provides for you.

That's the beauty of my Income Method. That's the beauty of income investing.

data.title: 3% Yield And Double-Digit Growth: Why Prologis Remains One Of The Best REITs Money Can Buy
data.description:
Introduction

By now, I doubt it's a surprise to most readers that I like companies with large footprints in critical supply chains. This includes Prologis (NYSE:PLD), a company I called "A Shareholder's Dream" in the title of my most recent article on July 8.

Since then, shares have returned 8%, beating the S&P 500 by roughly 270 basis points. Over the past ten years, it has returned 318%, making it one of the few REITs that beat the market. It also "crushed" the 95% return of the Vanguard Real Estate ETF (VNQ).

Prologis, which is the world's largest public warehouse owner, does not have a moat. In fact, none of the industrial REITs have a moat, as it is not hard to build warehouses. The same applies to many other REITs. Capital is often the only major factor that keeps people from building certain assets.

We view Prologis’ facilities as among the best in terms of quality and location, with a concentrated presence that closely aligns with regions representing the lowest capitalization rates in the country, such as Chicago, Miami, Dallas, Seattle, and coastal California. However, while Prologis’ facilities may be newer and well-located, nothing in them is difficult for competitors to replicate. - Morningstar

Nonetheless, Prologis has done many things right, including using its high-quality warehouses to become a key landlord to some of the world's largest companies. This includes Amazon, its biggest tenant. Going into this year, the online retailer occupied 34 million square feet and was responsible for 6.4% of PLD's net effective rent.

Other major players include Home Depot, FedEx, UPS, Geodis, Walmart, DHL, PepsiCo, Maersk, GXO, Ryder, and others. While most of these companies operate in different industries, they all have one thing in common: they require ultra-efficient supply chains to remain competitive.

Moreover, as the Morningstar quote showed, location is key. The company obviously knows it is dealing with elevated competition and low barriers to entry.

Hence, it focuses on markets where it is a bit easier to compete, looking for high-demand areas where it can gain somewhat of a competitive edge. This includes Southern California, Chicago, the NorthEast, East Texas, and a number of high-demand areas in Europe and Asia.

To give you two examples, Southern California is home to the ports of Los Angeles and Long Beach, two of the most important ports in the United States. In general, Southern California is the fourth-largest industrial real estate market on the planet.

Meanwhile, Chicago is America's most important railroad hub. Having assets in these markets makes the company a critical player for its tenants and the regions it serves.

In fact, the company is so large that the equivalent of 2.8% of global GDP flows through its distribution centers each year.

Even better, because it focuses on some of the strongest and fastest-growing tenants, less than a third of its customers are cyclical players. The remaining operations are either anti-cyclical or supported by secular growth trends like e-commerce.

This is yet another benefit for PLD in a moat-less industry and in light of economic volatility.

It also brings me to the main part of this article. Prologis reported earnings a few days ago, which is a great opportunity to re-assess the risk/reward for investors and discuss what makes this company special.

So, as we have a lot more to discuss, let's dive in!

Finally, We Could Be Nearing A Bottom

Because of its size, Prologis earnings are followed by many investors, even the ones who have no intention of ever buying PLD stock. Thanks to its large footprint, Prologis is one of the best indicators for global industrial real estate.

As The Wall Street Journal headline below shows, 3Q24 was a success, as the company was able to narrow its outlook on an improving logistics property market.

The supply developments are extremely encouraging, as the post-pandemic surge in new construction is over. This supports future pricing power and occupancy stability.

Warehouse developers have pared back construction over the past two years in a slowing industrial real-estate market following rapid expansion fueled by surging e-commerce demand during the pandemic.

The amount of industrial real estate under construction in the third quarter fell to 309 million square feet, down 43% from the previous year and the steepest drop since 2008, according to real-estate services firm Cushman & Wakefield. - The Wall Street Journal

Unfortunately, the industry is also dealing with headwinds, including companies that have become more cautious after aggressively leasing new space in recent years. Some players like Home Depot are subleasing warehouse space. Others have decided to focus on growing using the real estate they currently operate.

Combining both supply tailwinds and demand developments, Cushman & Wakefield expect vacancy rates to peak next year, paving the road for higher growth in the industry.

With new supply fading and demand poised to rebound next year, we expect vacancy to peak at 6.7% midway through 2025 (still below the historical average) and then trend lower. - Cushman & Wakefield

With regard to Prologis, while it noted weakness in key markets like Southern California and a global decline in market rents of roughly 3%, it still managed an 84% rent increase in Southern California, which is the benefit of mark-to-market adjustments. Essentially, many rents have not been adjusted to aggressive market rent growth in recent years.

It also should not come as a surprise that the company remains bullish on its future, supported by ongoing strength in markets like Latin America, Houston, and Northern Europe.

Moreover, the company confirms what Cushman & Wakefield see, which is that vacancies are peaking.

Where's The Shareholder Value?

In light of what we discussed so far, the company's core funds from operations ("FFO") came in at $1.45, slightly above the company's expectations. It's up from $1.33 in the prior-year quarter, as total core AFFO rose by 10.5%.

Moreover, it maintained a 96.2% occupancy rate, roughly 300 basis points above the market average. The average occupancy fell by 20 basis points to 96.1%. Customer retention remained high.

It also achieved a 68% increase in net effective rent and a 44% increase in cash rent. Mark-to-market adjustments alone provided $90 million in net operating income growth.

Going forward, the company's mark-to-market potential is 34%, equivalent to $1.6 billion in net operating income. This also lowers bankruptcy risks. Some of the vacancies that resulted from customer bankruptcies resulted in a rent upside of 60% due to mark-to-market opportunities.

Speaking of bankruptcies, Prologis maintains a fantastic balance sheet, which allowed it to raise $4.6 billion in net debt. This debt had a weighted average interest rate of just 4.9% and an average maturity of nine years. As the 10-year yield on U.S. government debt is 4.1%, it shows how much confidence the market has in the company's debt.

Unsurprisingly, it has a credit rating of A, one of the best ratings in the REIT space.

A big part of its proceeds ($1.4 billion) went to third-party acquisitions in 2024, allowing the company to acquire more than 14 million square feet of assets at a 20% discount to replacement costs.

It also installed 54 megawatts of new energy systems, putting it on track to reach more than 600 megawatts of energy generation by the end of 2024 and 1 gigawatt by 2025. The fact that it has a lot of flat roofs in sunny regions makes it perfect for solar power generation.

As of March 31, 2024, it was the 2nd-largest player in the U.S. for on-site solar generation.

With all of this in mind, the company tightened its guidance, as we already briefly discussed. It now sees a full-year occupancy rate between 96.0% and 96.5%, supported by strong demand for logistics real estate. It also sees at least 6.5% same-store net operating income growth.

This bodes well for its dividend.

Even in light of macroeconomic struggles, Prologis hiked its dividend by 10.3% on February 22, as it knows it has enough growth opportunities to support a higher distribution.

Since then, it has been paying $0.96 per share per quarter, which translates to a yield of 3.1%. Although the dividend lags behind the 5-6% yields of some REITs, it comes with a five-year CAGR of 12.6%. For most REITs, it is close to impossible to compete with this kind of dividend growth.

Going forward, I expect dividend growth to remain elevated.

Valuation

Using the FactSet data in the chart below, analysts expect 13% and 12% adjusted FFO growth in 2025 and 2026, respectively. This would make 2024 the last year of the "normalization" that started in 2023.

Currently, PLD trades at a blended P/AFFO ratio of 27.9x, roughly two points above its long-term average. Applying its long-term average of 25.7x, we get a fair stock price target of $142, 16% above its current price.

Given this valuation and a favorable industry outlook, I remain bullish on PLD and expect it to beat both the S&P 500 and its REIT peers in the years ahead.

I'm also watching PLD and its Southern Californian peer, Rexford Industrial Realty (REXR), for a potential entry in the months ahead.

Takeaway

Prologis continues to impress with its ability to navigate a competitive, moat-less industry while delivering strong results.

The company's strategic focus on high-demand markets, like Southern California and Chicago, gives it an edge, and its top-tier tenants provide a level of stability that's rare in industrial real estate.

Moreover, with its mark-to-market rent potential and attractive growth opportunities, I remain bullish on PLD.

While the stock isn't deep-value, I believe the combination of solid dividend growth, high-quality assets, and favorable industry dynamics make it a strong contender to outperform the S&P 500 and its REIT peers in the years ahead.

Pros & Cons

Pros:

  • Resilient Growth: Prologis has a strong track record, with continued growth supported by high demand for logistics real estate, especially from e-commerce giants.
  • Prime Locations: Assets in high-demand regions like Southern California and Chicago give Prologis a strategic edge, improving pricing power and tenant retention.
  • High-Quality Tenants: Top-tier clients like Amazon, FedEx, and Walmart provide stability.
  • Dividend Growth: Prologis has consistently increased its dividend, supported by a solid balance sheet and strong cash flow growth potential.
  • Healthy Balance Sheet: The company has an A-rated balance sheet with the ability to issue debt at extremely attractive rates.

Cons:

  • Valuation Premium: The stock trades at a premium compared to its historical average, putting more emphasis on growth expectations.
  • No Competitive Moat: Industrial real estate lacks significant barriers to entry, meaning Prologis faces ongoing competition in a crowded market.
  • Cyclical Risks: Despite strong tenants, any slowdown in global trade or economic downturns could impact demand for logistics space.

data.title: SMH: More Of A Pullback Is Warranted Before It Is A Buy
data.description:
SMH Appears To Be Over Valued At Current Levels - Worsened By The Uncertainty Entering The Q3'24 Earning Season

We previously covered VanEck Semiconductor ETF (NASDAQ:SMH) in July 2024, discussing the robust generative AI trends, with the ETF's top holdings benefiting from the second wave of the cloud computing boom and undisputed AI chip market dominance.

Even so, we had cautioned readers on the potential volatility, attributed to the holding's elevated P/E valuations and the market's ongoing correction from high growth stocks, resulting in our Hold (Neutral) rating then.

SMH YTD ETF Price

Since then, the deep correction has occurred as expected, with the head/ shoulder pattern materializing and the SMH ETF pulling back by -23% to $215 at its worst - with the same moderation similarly observed in its ETFs peers to varying degrees.

Since then, most has had a robust recovery uptrend as the inflation cools and the labor market remains resilient - with it already triggering the Fed's outsized pivot by 50 basis point in the September 2024 FOMC meeting.

As one of the first SMH holdings to report earnings, Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (NYSE:TSM) has reported a double beat FQ3'24 earnings result, with revenues of $23.5B (+12.7% QoQ/ +38.9% YoY) and GAAP EPS of $1.94 (+31.2% QoQ/ +54.1% YoY).

With the Taiwan foundry proving itself to be irreplaceable in the intermediate term, as Samsung (OTCPK:SSNLF) apologizes and Intel (INTC) flounders, we can understand why SMH's recovery has been as swift as it has since early September 2024 - attributed to the 12.51% weighting of TSM in the ETF's holdings.

This is significantly aided by Nvidia's (NVDA) upcoming launch of Blackwell by Q4'24, with numerous hyperscalers already receiving their first deliveries.

If anything, the insatiable demand for NVDA's chips have already triggered Foxconn's (OTCPK:FXCOF) commitment to "building the largest GB200 production facility on the planet," with it signaling the long-term durability of AI accelerator demand.

Assuming a successful ramp up, we believe that NVDA is likely to report another beat and raise performance in the upcoming FQ3'25 earnings call in November 2024 - with it building upon the seven consecutive quarters of top/ bottom-line beats since FQ4'23.

Even so, it is apparent that the exuberance may not last long, as observed in the dip observed after ASML Holding's (ASML) poorly received FQ3'24 earning results, with the bookings of €2.63B (-53% QoQ/ +1.1% YoY) missing the consensus estimates of €5.39B (-3.7% QoQ/ +107.3% YoY).

Part of the headwinds are naturally attributed to the stricter export controls for advanced lithography equipment from September 2024 onwards, with it impacting ASML's robust sales in China thus far.

For reference, the management has previously highlighted a growing backlog of €39B in FQ2'24 (+2.6% QoQ/ +2.6% YoY), with China commanding "a little bit north of 20%" of those bookings.

While ASML may still apply for export authorization, at which the Netherlands government will be looking to process on a case-by-case basis, it is apparent that the market has opted to sell first and think later.

The latter has already contributed to the pullback observed in SMH, a natural development since ASML comprises 4.87% of the ETF's holdings while contributing to the wider uncertainty surrounding semiconductor stocks.

With the next inline to report earnings being Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) on October 29, 2024, readers may want to observe the ongoing market developments indeed.

This is especially since the market has been increasingly bearish on its prospects as observed in the consistent downgrades, as the Lisa-Su led company appears to lag behind the clear market leader, NVDA, during the generative AI marathon.

Assuming another disappointing performance, we may see AMD further drag down SMH's uptrend performance thus far, worsened by INTC's potentially underwhelming earnings call scheduled on October 31, 2024 - with both comprising a total 8.64% of the ETF's holdings.

So, Is SMH ETF A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

SMH 5Y ETF Price

For now, SMH has partly recovered from the July/ August 2024 market rotation, with the recent correction seemingly defended by the bulls at the $240s.

This is partly buoyed by the promising generative AI SaaS market demand, significantly aided by the sustained upgrades in NVDA's top/ bottom-line projections through FY2027.

On the other hand, SMH's TTM P/E valuation has risen drastically to 42.88x, up from July 2024 levels of 22.28x - with it well exceeding many of its ETF peers, including SPY at 29.78x, QQQ at 31.06x, SOXQ at 33.52x, XSD at 30.21x, and SOXX at 31.18x.

If anything, the two day correction by -5.3% further exemplifies its premium valuations, as the market over reacts to ASML's supposedly softer FQ3'24 performance and the intensifying trade war with China.

The same over valuation has also been observed in numerous generative AI SaaS players, including Palantir (PLTR), with it currently trading at eye-watering FWD P/E valuations of 119.33x, nearing the heights of hyper-pandemic levels - with it signaling a potentially lower return profile.

With the Q3'24 earnings season just starting, we believe that there may be more volatility in the near-term, attributed to NVDA's tougher YoY comparison despite TSM's robust numbers thus far.

While the generative AI trends appear to be promising, with it likely to support SMH's long-term upward trend, we believe that it may be more prudent to observe the developing situation for a little longer before pulling the trigger.

For now, we are also looking to establish a new position at its previous uptrend support levels of between $220s by early September 2024 and $235s by early October 2024, with those levels implying a ~10% downside from current levels.

As a result of the near-term uncertainty, we prefer to maintain our Hold (Neutral) rating for the SMH ETF and only deciding upon the materialization of our targeted Buy zone and/ or the development arising from the US elections in November 2024.

For now, it may be wiser to lean on prudence and patience.

data.title: Elastic: This Is A Dip Worth Buying (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
With the S&P 500 continuing to hover near all-time highs, it's more prudent now than ever to be a careful stock-picker and gravitate more toward names with individual catalysts that can help them outperform the S&P 500.

Elastic (NYSE:ESTC) has caught my eye recently. After fessing up to sales execution issues, the enterprise search and observability platform has dropped like a stone, turning YTD gains into nearly a 30% decline since the start of January. In my view, it's a great time to re-assess the bull case on this name.

I last wrote a neutral hold opinion on Elastic in June, when the stock was trading above $110. At the time, I had cheered the company's stronger growth, but called out a rich valuation as my main deterrent to investing in the stock. Now with the stock trading substantially lower, I'm willing to take a bite on this name and am upgrading Elastic to a buy rating.

Let's first discuss the core reason Elastic dropped. The company recently reorganized its sales teams to give more priority to enterprise customers, as Elastic continues its push upmarket. The changes happened hastily and, in the process, some deals slipped. This, on top of macro challenges in Europe, caused Elastic to drop its full-year growth outlook to 14% y/y growth, two points weaker than a prior view of 16%:

At the same time, however, we like that the company also brought up its pro forma operating margin guidance to 12.5%, 50bps higher than previously, and nudged up its EPS range. Looking longer term, here are the reasons to be optimistic on Elastic's prospects:

  • Multiproduct platform supporting a large global TAM - Elastic is a true "horizontal" software product that addresses a variety of use cases and one that has been adopted by major corporations. It estimates its global TAM at $78 billion, suggesting only ~1% current penetration. This TAM has grown significantly versus $45 billion at the time of Elastic's IPO in 2018.
  • Longer term AI tailwinds. Elastic notes that it will take time for GenAI use cases to make up a large chunk of Elastic's revenue, but the company has signed on several hundred customers onto its AI-facing products (as mentioned on a recent earnings call). This has, in part, prevented Elastic from seeing meaningful growth deceleration.
  • Upmarket push. Elastic is increasingly focusing its sales drive on larger customers, which have far more expansion potential. While this may cost the company a large initial outlay of sales dollars, the recurring revenue these customers can bring is meaningful.

Stay long here and use the dip as a buying opportunity: in my view, this is a rebound play with plenty of potential.

Q1 download

Let's now go through Elastic's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The fiscal Q1 (July quarter) earnings summary is shown below:

Despite the company's bearish commentary surrounding its sales execution and guidance cut, Elastic actually beat in-quarter results: which gives us some hope that the company is setting a low bar for itself to cross in FY25, and that after the Q1 tumble we're in for a smoother sequence of beat-and-raise quarters. Q1 revenue grew 18% y/y to $347.4 million, ahead of Wall Street's $344.6 million (+17% y/y) expectations.

It's helpful to review the anecdotal commentary that the company provided around sales execution and market trends. On the recent Q1 earnings call, CEO Ash Kulkarni noted as follows:

In terms of the issues that affected the total customer commitments closed in Q1, we were impacted by sales segmentation changes we made at the beginning of Q1 to increase focus on our strategic enterprise and high-potential mid-market customers (…)

However, we underestimated the impact of the account transitions that occurred with these changes. This was especially true in the Americas where we had the largest territory changes and we just didn't progress deals fast enough to bring them over the finish line.

Separately, and to a lesser degree, tighter customer budget constraints led to delays in closing deals in EMEA. None of these deals were lost and we expect to close these in due course. Working with Mark Dodds and his sales leadership team, I have significantly heightened the monitoring of deal progress in the areas where we had the biggest segmentation changes.

With this increased rigor, the deeper focus on our largest accounts and quite simply, as reps have had more time to cover their new accounts, we are already seeing encouraging signs of deals progressing through the sales funnel."

The macro comments that the company provided around Europe are echoed elsewhere throughout the industry. And we note as well that despite the very acidic post-earnings reaction, Elastic's revenue hasn't really decelerated meaningfully versus the prior few quarters as shown in the chart below. The "encouraging signs" in the sales funnel that management commented on give us further hope that revenue is on more solid footing than Elastic's share price may suggest.

Equally impressive are the company's profitability results. Pro forma operating margins rose 80bps y/y to 10.7%, as shown in the chart below.

We note that the company's guidance calls for margins to jump meaningfully to ~13% by next quarter (a reflection of delayed deals pushed from Q1 surfacing), which may give us further reason to believe the company's 12.5% margin guidance for the year also has room to slide up.

Valuation and key takeaways

At current share prices near $82, Elastic trades at an $8.49 billion market cap. After we net off the $1.15 billion of cash and $569 million of debt on Elastic's latest balance sheet, the company's resulting enterprise value is $7.91 billion.

This puts Elastic at a 5.5x EV/FY25 revenue multiple against the midpoint of the company's $1.436-$1.444 billion revenue guidance range for the year - whereas previously, Elastic had been able to command at least a high single digit revenue multiple.

Yes, it's true that Elastic now bears some sales execution risk, as the recent re-organization of its sales force and push upmarket may take time to bear fruit. Still, with the stock just coming off YTD lows, it's a great time to catch Elastic on the upswing.

data.title: Schlumberger: Growth May Be Slowing, But The Buybacks Are Accelerating
data.description:
Investment thesis

Schlumberger (NYSE:SLB), the oilfield services behemoth, reported a slight Q3 earnings beat Friday although topline revenue growth fell a bit below expectations. The market reaction wasn't favorable to say the least:

With the exception of the well-known weakness in North America onshore, the company posted improving metrics across most of its other markets. Perhaps the part the market didn't like were these comments from the CFO during the earnings call:

[On] 2025 and the absolute value of EBITDA compared to our long-term '21 to '25 CAGR ambition, it is possible that indeed, with the current macro outlook again to be refined, and [if we exclude] ChampionX, it's possible that the '21 to '25 CAGR will finish in the high teens rather than breaking that 20% bar.

The relative deceleration is a negative for sure, but in absolute terms growth remains very high and likely more than what was ever priced in the stock. In addition, SLB's profitability has already grown to a level where free cash flow generation has inflected and the company is now accelerating its buybacks:

[Total] returns to shareholders, in the form of stock repurchases and dividends, was approximately $2.4 billion on a year-to-date basis. During the third quarter, we repurchased 11.3 million shares for a total purchase price of $501 million. As a result of our strong cash flow performance, we expect to maintain this level of buyback in the fourth quarter.

Consequently, we will exceed our previous commitment to return $3 billion to our shareholders in 2024. Furthermore, we reaffirm that we will return a minimum of $4 billion to shareholders in 2025, reflecting our confidence in our ability to continue generating strong cash flows.

This means we are looking at a minimum distribution yield of 6.5% for 2025. In my view, the cash flow generation will be more important in the near term as the marginal buyer for oilfield services stocks appear to be the companies themselves through buybacks. Growth is important to attract new investors but the reality is that capital has already been flowing out of the energy services sector for 3 years now:

Lastly, the ChampionX acquisition now set to close in Q1 2025 is expected to contribute synergies which aren't yet reflected in the 2024 reporting.

The Q3 performance was solid

SLB's revenue growth YoY was still in the double digits for the more important international market:

The Middle East in particular, which spooked investors in January after the Saudi abandonment of its prior expansion plans, is still growing at 16% annual rate. North America is a known variable so probably the slowness in LATAM is the only surprise here.

The quarterly EBITDA margin was north of 25% and EBITDA growth continues to outpace revenue:

EBITDA conversion to free cash flow at about 80% was exceptionally strong in Q3. This implies a free cash flow margin on revenue of close to 20%.

Free cash flow is a volatile metric due to timing issues but the trend over the past several quarters is clear:

Generally, when the fortunes of a business improve, revenue tends to lead, followed by EBITDA and finally cash flow. I think for SLB 2024 is the year when we finally see the revenue growth from the past couple years starting to trickle down to the free cash flow line.

The buybacks are just starting

An important point that probably got missed by yesterday's headlines is that the growth is no longer capex fueled, especially on the side of the promising digital business:

[Our] growth in digital doesn't trigger incremental capital intensity because it's really smooth over many, many years. I would say that the heavy investments have been made in the past already now is just enriching the platform and supporting the enriched offering for the customer.

This bodes well for buybacks and Q3 was also a record quarter in that regard:

It has really been just 3 quarters so far with material buybacks and that will only get better in 2025. The minimum $4 billion distribution target, assuming $1.6 billion will go dividends, implies a minimum buyback of $600 million per quarter.

Also perhaps by design, perhaps by luck - SLB didn't buy back much in 2023 when the stock price was in the $60s; instead we are seeing the firepower deployed now when SLB trades in the low $40s:

The good timing of these buybacks implies greater effect on EPS down the road. The ChampionX acquisition probably also proved fortuitous in this regard as the deal was announced when SLB stock was comfortably in the $50s.

Price targets remain optimistic

Analysts usually slash down their targets if the stock underperforms their expectations but on SLB they have remained stubbornly bullish:

I find the targets range, from a $50 bear case to $70 bullish scenario, to be reasonable. Even if CAGR drops from 20% to the "high teens" at $41 entry price SLB looks to be a safe bet. All this assumes oil remains around $70 too so we are not betting on any type of "commodity super-cycle" here.

Takeaway

Energy services is a cyclical industry so growth having ups and downs shouldn't surprise anyone. The important takeaway for SLB investors is that in less than 4 years since the pandemic the company has managed to achieve record profitability and is already generating significant free cash flow. Even with lower topline growth, the cash returns are set to increase and that will be a tailwind going into 2025.

data.title: Where To Invest $100,000 For Retirement With Big Dividends
data.description:
Introduction:

The markets have had an incredible past two years. Since the beginning of 2023, the S&P 500 has gained roughly 27%. The Nasdaq 100, which is heavy in the technology sector, has gained even more at 42%. In fact, broadly speaking, the last 10 years have been a wonderful time to be invested in the stock market. Has it always been like that? The simple answer is no. It depends on which period you are looking at. If you look at the 10-year period from January 2000 to December 2009, the S&P 500 actually lost about 1% on an annual basis, while the Nasdaq-100 lost nearly 6.5% annualized. However, in the long run, the stock market has always gone up, comfortably meeting and beating inflation and the rise in the cost of living.

So, the more pertinent question will be this. Is it a good time to invest today? Let’s say you got $100,000 to invest. You may be rolling your 401K into an IRA, or you got some inheritance to deploy, or maybe you sold your business at some profit and have spare money to invest. Whatever the reason, what should you do with the idle cash and where should you invest right now? We know that sitting in cash (one that you do not need for a few years) is never a good idea. Sure, interest rates have been high for some time and still can get you between 4% and 5% if you're keeping it in money-market accounts or CDs. However, interest rates have started coming down, and we're going to get less and less income as we head into 2025. Also, frankly, 4.5% does not excite us at all, as most of it will be eaten up by taxes and inflation, with nothing left to grow. It does not look like a very good prospect to us.

So, let’s see where we can invest our spare capital today to earn at least 10% at cost on an annual basis after an initial accumulation period of 10 years.

Why Dividends Are Ideal For Retirement:

So, why are dividends so important for a comfortable retirement? Obviously, there are two camps: One that loves income and dividend investing. It also has a large following on Seeking Alpha. The other camp, which does not believe in the power of dividend investing, usually offers a sharp critique of the dividend approach. They have many arguments against dividend investing. First, that income can be raised by selling shares on a regular basis, so why bother about dividends? Second, they claim that dividend investing leads to poor selection of stocks. There's always something to learn from opposing views, so their arguments should not be washed away without any thought. We can always learn and improve from those views. Sure, if you're many years or decades away from retirement and are in a relatively stable profession, then the utility or usefulness of dividend investing is limited, and you could focus more on compounding your capital rather than the income.

With all that said, this article focuses on dividend growth investing, and certainly, we belong to the dividend/income camp. As we can see, the biggest advantage of dividend investing is having a relatively worry-free retirement. Folks work all their adult life while saving for retirement. When it finally arrives and assuming you have saved a reasonable sum for your portfolio to afford you enough income, it is time to enjoy the retirement and the extra time that it gives you. You do not want to be sitting with your laptop and calculator all the time wondering how much money to raise and what the best time to sell your shares to raise that income is.

How To Ensure Growth Without Taking Undue Risks:

Now, dividend investing does not and should not mean only income or income at the cost of growth. The reason we are investing in the stock market is that it can provide growth that outpaces inflation by a good margin over the long term. So, if we are an income or dividend investor, it does not mean our income should come at the cost of growth. Sure, if we are in the withdrawal phase, our capital can’t grow at the same pace as it would if we were in the accumulation phase (reinvesting all the income back).

The goal is that we draw enough income to meet all our expenses and reinvest everything else back into our investment portfolio. Our general recommendation is to withdraw up to 5% income (6% as a maximum limit), and any excess income should be reinvested back into the portfolio. This will allow our portfolio to prosper, beat inflation, and grow significantly over a long period of time. This growth will allow the owners to pass on a significant sum to their heirs or to their chosen causes, whatever the case may be.

However, if someone has very limited capital and their goal is to use most of their money during their lifetime, then a withdrawal rate in excess of 6% could be considered. But this definitely comes with some risk of outliving your money.

The Dividend Portfolio for Retirement:

We believe that our articles should always offer some investment ideas or strategies that folks could use. We do not recommend following our portfolios exactly the way they are presented, as everyone’s situation is unique. However, one could take the basic framework from our article and adjust or alter it to suit their unique needs and risk profile.

So, here's one nearly complete portfolio that anyone could use as the base. Our usual investment strategies almost always include multiple buckets. But, in today’s article, we will keep it simple and limit it to just one bucket.

We will structure our portfolio around five funds and 10 individual stocks that could be attractive today.

The portfolio will consist of

  • 5 Income Funds:

Schwab US Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD),

Eaton Vance Enhanced Equity Inc Fund II (EOS),

Adams Diversified Equity Fund Inc (ADX),

Fidelity Capital & Income Fund (FAGIX),

Adams Natural Resources Fund Inc (PEO).

  • 10 Individual Dividend Stocks:

Microsoft Corp (MSFT),

PepsiCo Inc (PEP),

Union Pacific Corp (UNP),

Realty Income Corp (O),

Mid-America Apartment Communities (MAA),

Main Street Capital Corp (MAIN),

Visa Inc (V),

Texas Instruments Inc (TXN),

Verizon Communications Inc. (VZ),

Enbridge Inc (ENB)..

Table-1: Portfolio of 5 Funds and 10 Stocks

Retirement Portfolio Analysis with Charts

Sector Allocation:

We can see a fair level of sector diversification. However, technology appears to be a bit overweight at 21%, but it's still very reasonable compared to 32% of the S&P 500. Also, the fund EOS is contributing to the high weighting of the technology sector, but EOS has options overwritten to the extent of nearly 50%.

Chart-1: Sector Allocation

Risk Analysis:

Here, we will compare how much each fund/security contributes to the risk compared to its allocation and return. The period for this analysis is from 2012 to 2024. For example, if a fund or security has an allocation of 10%, anything less than 10% of risk contribution will be lower risk than normal (its allocation), and anything more than 10% risk will be a higher risk contribution on that scale.

Table-2: Return Vs Risk Contribution

We can see that the fund FAGIX (a bond fund) has a 10% allocation, but the risk contribution is just 5.7%. However, it also has a much lower return contribution of 5.4%. On the other hand, a stock like MSFT had double the return contribution (compared to allocation), while the risk contribution was slightly lower than its allocation.

Yield at Cost in 10 Years:

Now, the average dividend growth of this portfolio in the last five years has been roughly 7.5%. With the initial yield of 5.4%, this should grow to roughly 10% in 10 years, assuming we get similar growth in the next 10 years. Obviously, these are assumptions, and past record is no guarantee of similar results in the future.

Assuming you plan to retire in 10 years, this portfolio with an initial investment of $100,000 should throw roughly $10,000 in annual dividend income. Also, if you estimate that you would need an income of $50,000 from your savings at retirement, you would need to invest roughly $500,000. These are just rough calculations for guidance, and you should do due diligence based on your situation. That said, we believe this portfolio with five funds and 10 stocks offers a highly balanced, diversified, and relatively safe way to plan for retirement.

Please see the table below for details.

Table-3: Yield at cost in 10 years

Note: The dividend growth for ADX and PEO is an estimate as their dividend policies have recently changed. Their distributions will now be linked with their NAV. The growth rates for other securities are sourced from Seeking Alpha and other sources.

Concluding Thoughts:

We have presented a highly diversified and balanced portfolio that anyone can implement easily. It also provides investors with the flexibility to change a few of the stock selections as per their needs and preferences without changing the overall framework. The portfolio offers a high initial yield of nearly 5.4% with the potential to grow the income at roughly 7% - 8% annually. In addition to the high income, there's no compromise on growth, and the portfolio offers SWAN (sleep well at night) like characteristics.

data.title: PEJ: Thematic ETF With Good Fundamentals, But Bad Track Record
data.description:
PEJ strategy

Invesco Leisure and Entertainment ETF (NYSEARCA:PEJ) was launched on 06/23/2005 and tracks the Dynamic Leisure & Entertainment Intellidex℠ Index. It has 32 holdings and a total expense ratio of 0.58%. As described in the prospectus by Invesco, the underlying index selects companies that…

...are engaged principally in the design, production or distribution of goods or services in the leisure and entertainment industries. These companies may include hospitality industry companies such as hotels, restaurants and bars, cruise lines, casinos, and all other recreation and amusement businesses; as well as entertainment programming companies engaged in the production of motion pictures, music by recording artists, programming for radio and television, related post-production and movie theaters.

The portfolio turnover rate in the most recent fiscal year was 163%, which is very high for such a thematic fund.

PEJ portfolio

The portfolio has about 8% of asset value in large companies, 52% in mid-caps and 41% in small and micro-caps. About 50% of assets is in the consumer discretionary sector and 36% in communication services. PEJ also has marginal exposure in consumer staples and industrials.

At sub-sector level in the GICS classification, all the Consumer Discretionary weight is in a single category: Hotels, Restaurants & Leisure. The Communication Services part is concentrated in the Entertainment industry (28%), with also minor exposure in Media and Interactive Media & Services. The Consumer Staples part is exclusively in distribution and retail, while airlines make all of the small allocation in industrials.

The portfolio is quite concentrated. The top 10 holdings, listed in the next table, represent 47% of asset value and the four heaviest positions weigh between 5% and 6%.

Fundamentals

The next table compares valuation and growth metrics of PEJ and a broad stock benchmark: iShares Core S&P Total US Stock Market ETF (ITOT). Both funds have almost the same price/earnings ratio, but PEJ is significantly cheaper based on price/book, prices/sales and price/cash flow. It also shows superior sales and cash flow growth rates.

Moreover, the heaviest industry in the portfolio (Hotels, Restaurants & Leisure) is part of the consumer services category, which has good value and quality scores in my monthly Consumer Discretionary dashboard. In summary, PEJ fundamentals are quite compelling compared to broad market ETFs, and sector ETFs as well.

Performance

PEJ historical performance is more problematic: it has underperformed the U.S. stock market by over 3% in annualized return since 7/1/2005. Additionally, risk metrics (volatility and drawdown) are significantly higher, as reported below.

PEJ is also slightly behind the broad market over the last 12 months:

Takeaway

Invesco Leisure and Entertainment ETF (PEJ) holds a portfolio of about 30 companies providing leisure and entertainment products and services. Hospitality and communication industries represent 86% of its asset value. PEJ fundamental metrics are compelling, but its track record is underwhelming, while the fee and turnover look very high for this kind of thematic ETF. The edge of PEJ is in its unique concept. I am not aware of another ETF mixing hospitality and communication in a leisure and entertainment theme (if you know one, feel free to comment below).

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: China Automotive Systems: Revenue And Profitability Growth Not Converting To Cash Flows
data.description:
China Automotive Systems, Inc. (NASDAQ:CAAS) has seen its share price recover in 2024, following the sharp decline last year.

Despite the steady revenue growth since 2021, the company hasn’t been able to turn this improvement into stronger cash flows, which naturally, raised some eyebrows.

Therefore, in this article, I considered reviewing the recent financial performance of the company, with an emphasis on the headwinds, challenges, and risks that the company is facing.

In the outlook section, I will provide the rationale behind my Hold rating, including a deep dive into valuation and the fundamentals of the company.

For now, I would like to start with a brief company overview section for those readers new to this stock.

Company Overview

China Automotive, as its name suggests, is a Chinese based supplier of power steering systems for the automotive industry. Their power gears are used in passenger vehicles and commercial trucks.

Their major customers include large automotive manufacturers in China (BYD Auto, Geely, and Chery) and international companies like Stellantis and Ford.

The company operates mainly in China through several subsidiaries that specialize in different areas of the automotive steering market. I considered including a breakdown of revenue per business segment below.

Ownership-wise, the latest 14A shows 65% ownership of common stock across all 9 directors and executive officers. In my view, a very respectable amount of skin in the game.

Headwinds

I'd like to start with dessert.

One of the factors that raised my eyebrow in their last earnings results was the disconnection between their strong revenue growth and the decline in net income.

Net sales were up by 15.4% YoY, operating income was up by 28%, however, net income declined by 32% in the same period, falling from $10.5 million to $7.1 million.

By looking at the income statement, it seems that this decline was driven by unfavorable foreign exchange rates.

The company reported a net financial expense of $0.7 million, a big contrast to the net financial income of $4.0 million in Q2 2023.

In other words, we are talking about a $4.7 million negative swing in the company’s financial results due to foreign exchange volatility, which is quite concerning, in my view.

The company has substantial exposure to foreign currencies, due to its revenue from export markets like North America and Brazil.

Looking at the charts of the Brazilian Real vs the Chinese Yuan Renminbi and the US Dollar vs Chinese Yuan Renminbi the company's foreign exchange loss in Q2 makes sense.

Despite this high volatility, I remain highly disappointed that the company has no foreign exchange forward contracts to hedge against foreign exchange risks. Back in 2023, the company entered into foreign exchange forward contracts to mitigate the impact of exchange rate fluctuations between the RMB and USD. However, this strategy has not been extended to 2024, leaving the company exposed to exchange rate fluctuations.

Moving on to other pressures, operating expenses increased by 40.7% YoY. I am not overly concerned about this increase considering that operating income saw a decent improvement in the last quarter of 28.9%.

However, I am slightly more concerned about the risks associated with their transition towards electric power systems. As of today, the traditional steering product market is still a large portion of the company's revenue, accounting for over 65% of net sales.

However, as the industry gradually moves towards more electrified and autonomous vehicles, the demand for traditional steering components may stagnate or decline, particularly in more advanced markets like North America and Europe.

That's not the situation today, since sales of the traditional systems grew by 7.5% compared to last year. However, in the next 5-10 years, the company may need to shift its focus from traditional power systems to electric ones. Otherwise, their revenue could take a significant hit.

Outlook

I'd like to start with price action.

If we look at the share price over the last 2 years, there is a mixed performance, with a significant increase in Q1 2023, followed by a decline in the same year.

From a technical analysis perspective, there is an upward trend line that seems to be quite reliable.

From a valuation perspective, most of the valuation ratios seem to be trading close to the 5-year minimum, except for P/CF and P/FCF.

So, let's have a closer look at the cash flow statement and try to understand why these two ratios look so bad.

Operating cash flow dropped by 200% in the second quarter, but what's even more worrying is that free cash flow plunged by 700%.

As mentioned in the previous section, the increase in operating expenses and the decline in financial income due to unfavorable forex rates played a big role in this decline.

In addition, accounts and notes receivable saw a significant increase of $20.5 million. I am concerned about this increase in receivables, as the revenue growth is not leading to an increase in cash, indicating either extended credit terms to customers or delays in collecting payments.

On top of that, income taxes paid went up by $600k compared to last year, adding to the overall decline.

If we zoom out, and we consider a yearly timeframe, the trend in operating and free cash flow does not look favorable, despite the revenue growth.

My investment style doesn't align with this trend since I put more emphasis on cash flow than revenue, except for companies in their early growth phase.

Moving on to the balance sheet, the company has negative net debt, with cash & equivalents sitting at $148.4 million.

The liquidity ratios look ok. Nothing spectacular, but no major red flags.

Ratio Value
Current Ratio 1.5
Quick Ratio 1.2
Cash Ratio 0.4

The cash ratio is low, mainly due to the $162 million in accounts payable.

Moving on to insider buying activity, over the past 12 months there was only one transaction made by the CFO, Li Jie, totaling $19k in November 2023.

Considering the small amount purchased by the CFO, and the lack of recent insider buying, I remain disappointed. On the bright side, there were no insider selling transactions.

The activist activity has been silent since 2018, and the amount of shares owned by institutional investors has not changed drastically in the last quarter. However, the put/call ratio increased by 36% in Q2, indicating some pessimism among 13F filers.

Conclusion

To wrap up, I find that China Automotive presents a mixed outlook.

On the bright side, the company has shown solid revenue growth consistently since 2021. Operating income followed this trend, however, the net income of the company seems to be quite volatile due to foreign exchange rates.

I appreciate the fact that management has a significant ownership stake in the company, but I disagree with their decision not to hedge against foreign exchange fluctuations in 2024.

On the negative side, the 700% YoY drop in free cash flow, despite revenue growth, points out to operational inefficiencies, particularly with increasing accounts receivable. Additionally, their transition to electric power systems remains a concern, especially if the company fails to pivot toward electrification as the automotive industry moves in that direction.

On top of that, the lack of insider buying and the increasing put/call ratio shows that both management and institutional investors are being cautious.

To conclude, while I see decent revenue growth, I don’t see the same improvement in their cash flow statement. Therefore, I’m keeping a Hold rating until their cash flows improve and, ideally, insiders start buying shares.

data.title: Johnson & Johnson: Q3 Earnings Highlights Resilience And New Growth
data.description:
It has been nearly a year since my last Johnson & Johnson (NYSE:JNJ) “J&J” article, where I highlighted their Q3 2023 earnings that beat the Street’s projections with $21.4B in revenue, which was up 6.8% year-over-year. At that time, it looked as if the company’s MedTech division was going to be a primary growth driver, along with Darzalex and Abiomed. That quarter was a critical time for J&J following their strategic spin-off of Kenuve consumer health business while still dealing with the ongoing Talc litigation. In addition, J&J was facing the looming threat of STELARA biosimilar competition for one their blockbuster drugs. Despite the murky outlook, I remained optimistic thanks to J&J’s pipeline and their track record of excellence. Now, we are here a year later looking at their Q3 earnings report that revealed a beat on EPS and revenue that was once again fueled by the company's blockbusters Darzalex, Invega Sustenna, and STELARA. That being said, the company has adjusted their full-year earnings guidance following their V-Wave acquisition for $1.7B amongst other headwinds. After a brief sell-off, JNJ was able to recover in the trading session and is now trading above my Buy Threshold of $163 per share. Although I am still apprehensive about the impact of STELARA generics in the coming quarters, I believe their Q3 earnings showed the company’s resilience and some new growth opportunities. As a result, I am looking to add to my JNJ position in the near term.

I intend to review the company’s Q3 earnings and will provide my views on the quarter. Then, I will highlight some of J&J's new growth opportunities. In addition, I will point out some notable risks investors need to be aware of. Finally, I reveal my plans for managing my JNJ in the coming days and weeks.

Q3 Earnings Review

Taking a look at J&J’s Q3 earnings their total revenue came in at $22.5B, which was up 5.4% year-over-year. In terms of EPS, J&J publicized that their non-GAAP EPS came in at $2.42. The company attributed their growth to the Innovative Medicine segment, which pulled in $14.58B, up 0.8% quarter-over-quarter and 4.9% year-over-year. I will highlight that once again, STELARA was able to beat analyst expectations and was the primary contributor throwing $2.68B into the coffers.

Again, J&J’s Darzalex was a heavy hitter posting $3.02B for Q3, which beat the Street's forecast of $2.94B. In addition, other oncology drugs such as ARVYKTI, TECVAYLI, and TALVEY are showing growth. ARVYKTI hit $286M in sales, which was up 87.6% year-over-year and 53.2% quarter-over-quarter. In addition, TECVAYLI has made strong gains in the multiple myeloma market with sales growing 21.4% year-over-year to hit $135M. J&J also highlighted ERLEADA’s growth, up 26.3% year-over-year, as it continues to claim more of the metastatic castrate-sensitive prostate cancer market.

Meanwhile, immunology had mixed results, with TREMFYA posting 14.3% growth as it makes inroads into the psoriasis and psoriatic arthritis markets. The company’s neuroscience products are also contributing with SPRAVATO recording 55.3% growth. J&J’s OPSUMIT and UPTRAVI posted solid growth of 17.4% and 15.2%, respectively.

In addition, the company’s MedTech segment recorded a solid performance as the number of procedures increased and they rolled out new products. MedTech hit $7.89B in sales, growing 0.8% quarter-over-quarter increase and 5.2% year-over-year. The company reported a recovery in the number of surgical procedures driving demand for their medical devices. Although the segment did miss the Street’s mark of $8.03B, the growing demand does bode well for J&J’s MedTech business going forward. In addition, J&J pointed to their acquisitions of Shockwave and Abiomed helped drive double-digit growth.

Turning to the company’s vision products, ACUVUE OASYS’s price adjustments led to 4.7% growth, and their TECNIS products saw some growth.

Looking at J&J’s regional sales figures, their U.S. sales were scribed in at $12.57B, which was up 2.7% from Q2 and 7.6% year-over-year. On the other hand, the company pulled in $9.88B from international markets, which was down 1.2% from last quarter, but up 2.2% year-over-year. Notably, the company believes the drop from Q2 was mostly due to currency and weaker sales in Europe. However, the company did point out that the year-over-year growth came from emerging markets grabbing Innovative Medicine and MedTech products.

The company’s Q3 earnings encouraged them to update their full-year 2024 guidance with sales coming in between $89.4B-$89.8B, which is up from $89.2B-$89.6B. J&J also updated their adjusted operational EPS guidance to $9.86-$9.96, which is down from $10.00-$10.10. I must point out that the company said they revised their EPS due to the V-Wave acquisition, and talc litigation costs.

In terms of their finances, J&J closed out Q3 with ~$20B in cash and marketable securities thanks to $14B in free cash flow. In addition, the company has about $36B in debt, leading to a net debt of about $16B.

My Take

For me, J&J’s Q3 results highlighted their ability to frequently outperform the Street’s expectations, despite some of the looming concerns and operating in a challenging environment. Beating the Street’s EPS estimates by $0.21 and surpassing revenue forecasts by $330M shows just how their leading products are in control of their markets, helping the company log $22.5B in total revenue for the quarter. STELARA and Darzalex are the company’s big hitters who are still capable of carrying the company forward. However, I’m also encouraged by their MedTech segment, which posted a solid 5.2% year-over-year growth. I am also encouraged about the company’s outlook on how their Shockwave and Abiomed acquisitions could have a strong impact in the near future.

Indeed, J&J still has plenty of challenges including generics, currency headwinds, and ongoing litigation, but I see J&J's decision to adjust their guidance and maintain strong cash flow as strong signals that they are optimistic about their near-term performance. Furthermore, the fact that they’re still paying out $8.8B in dividends year-to-date reassures me they’re committed to shareholders while not stunting growth.

Overall, I don’t see any major red flags coming out this earnings, and the company’s beat should tell you that the lingering concerns are not hindering their performance.

Looking For Growth

J&J's Darzalex and STELARA, have set a solid foundation for the company to continue to grow in the near term. However, I have to recognize that the upcoming generic competition for STELARA could deteriorate branded sales, faster than Darzalex can grow. Thankfully, I believe that J&J’s overall pharmaceutical portfolio still has pending growth. The company’s CARVYKTI and TECVALYI have reported strong double-digit year-over-year growth and are positioned to grab more of the oncology market. I’m also excited about some of J&J’s new products and their ability to be immediate contributors. I must also acknowledge that RYBREVANT and TREMFYA are now approved and could bulk up their oncology and immunology revenues. I'm also champing at the bit to see the sales disclosures for TALVEY, as well as the anticipated sales for RYBREVANT.

Although J&J's MedTech segment slightly underperformed in Q3, it still holds a lot of promise with their surgical systems and cardiovascular devices, which should deliver significant sales in the future. The ongoing integration of V-Wave’s cardiac device technologies should start to show traction after adding some short-term pressure to earnings. The company should also see some revenue growth from REMICADE and SIMPONI in Europe as distribution rights are set to return to J&J in Q4 of this year.

I am not the only one expecting growth from J&J… the Street is expecting the company to report growth in the coming quarters with a solid single-digit to low double-digit revenue growth from Q4 2024 to Q4 2026.

Admittedly, the quarterly EPS estimates are expected to stagnate for a few quarters, but analysts are projecting the numbers to improve in Q3 of next year and will continue to grow through 2026.

Zooming out, we can see that analysts are expecting J&J to report single-digit growth for the remainder of the decade for both EPS and revenue.

Certainly, single-digit growth is not going to justify a premium valuation, however, I think it does repudiate the narrative that STELARA generics and the talc litigation are going derail J&J’s growth. Yes, both of those issues have most likely hurt the company’s growth trajectory, but the company can still leverage their pharmaceutical strengths, innovative therapies, and strategic acquisitions to drive growth for the remainder of the decade.

Risks To Consider

Despite my bullish outlook for J&J, I still have to acknowledge a few risks that could hurt the company's long-term outlook. First, I must acknowledge their talc litigation, which will most likely continue to weigh on the ticker as investors remain apprehensive about the fallout from potential settlements.

Furthermore, the costs of integrating V-Wave are going to be a concern. While this acquisition helped contribute to the top line, it has also increased expenses. The company is still expecting an impact in 2025, but any setbacks in rollouts and R&D could hurt profitability. As a result, J&J needs to show that they can quickly and seamlessly integrate V-Wave into their existing operations in order to start hitting the Street’s expectations for their MedTech segment.

Last but not least, the impending generic competition for STELARA is a serious concern. In fact, the drug’s Q3 sales experienced a decline of 5.7% due to biosimilar competition in Europe, and investors should expect a similar challenge in the U.S. in 2025. Investors should be prepared for STELARA sales numbers to dip, which will require the company to rely heavily on their newer therapies to fill the gap and maintain the growth trajectory.

Despite these risks, I maintain my conviction level for J&J at a strong 5 out of 5. I believe the company has the resilience and strategic vision to navigate these challenges effectively. Furthermore, I still view J&J as a “Healthy Dividend” stock, reflecting its commitment to returning capital to shareholders even amidst these headwinds.

My Plan

Indeed, the launch of STELARA generics is inevitable and will be a major blow to the company's portfolio. In addition, the dark cloud from the talc litigation will likely prevent the ticker from outperforming its peers. It is possible that STELARA is able to fend off generics longer than Street believes, and maybe the talc litigation will finally come to an end sooner than expected. Moreover, the company's next batch of growth drivers might be able to help offset some of the projected drop in revenue from STELARA. Plus, it looks as if Darzalex will continue to be the main driver in the near term with projected peak sales of $13B to roughly $16B by 2029. As a result, I will be looking to add to my JNJ position in the near term if the ticker is able to drop below my “Buy Threshold” of $163 per share.

Looking at the Daily Chart, we can see JNJ has rebounded from support around $161 and is approaching resistance near $165. The chart shows several indicators are pointing to a potential move higher now that the share price has climbed above the anchored-VWAP and the “Go-No-Go” is now neutral. On the other hand, the TW Pivot is suggesting a potential bearish reversal is on the horizon, so I am going to wait to see if the ticker can break above the downtrend ray and will retest support before clicking the buy button. If that occurs, I will make an upsized addition and then will revisit the ticker at the end of the month to see if I have to update my Buy and Sell Targets.
